Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered because I'm juggling a lot at 29 with limited resources. I'd usually carve out blocks on weekends and a couple of evenings when I could focus, aiming for around 18 hours a week. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to actually do things with my hands—write notes, work through problems physically—so a lot of my studying happened away from the screen. I'd download what I needed and then work through it on paper at the kitchen table or even at work during breaks.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, but then work would get hectic or something would come up financially, and I'd have to shift everything around. Deadlines were the main driver—I'd ramp up my hours significantly when assignments were approaching. It wasn't ideal, but it's what worked given my circumstances.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or seeing if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in to grab lecture materials or see if there were any announcements I'd missed. I wasn't one to browse around the LMS for fun—I went in with a specific purpose and got out. With four online courses, I couldn't afford to waste time.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it always felt like a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my daily life. I'd sometimes set reminders on my phone to check in weekly, but that felt more like a chore than a routine. The mindfulness practice I picked up actually helped me be more intentional about when I did log in rather than making it more frequent.
Q5: The downloadable materials were gold for me—PDFs, lecture slides, anything I could print or save to work with offline. I also found the assignment submission portal straightforward, which mattered when you're rushing before a deadline. The discussion boards were occasionally useful when I needed clarification from peers, though I didn't post as much as some others. Anything hands-on or practical felt most valuable to my learning style.
Q6: I largely ignored the videos beyond skimming them, even though I watched a fair amount of minutes according to the system. I'd often have them playing while doing other things, which isn't really engaging. Optional readings and supplementary materials usually got pushed aside—with limited time and four courses, I had to prioritize what directly impacted my grades. Some of the discussion prompts felt repetitive, so I'd skip those too.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or helped me understand something I'd use on the exam. Practical applications were key—if I could see how something would help me in real situations, I was much more invested. Abstract theoretical discussions that didn't seem to lead anywhere concrete just felt like busywork, especially when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Absolutely different. Required activities got done no matter what—those were non-negotiable because they affected my grade. Optional stuff? That was the first thing cut when time got tight, which was often. I'd tell myself I'd come back to the optional materials later, but honestly, that rarely happened. It's not that I didn't see their value; I just couldn't justify the time investment when basics needed covering first.
Q9: Oh, constantly. I'd download lecture slides and readings, print them out when I could afford to, and work through everything with a pen in hand. I had notebooks full of handwritten notes and practice problems that the LMS would never see. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what we were learning—sometimes external explanations clicked better for me. My actual studying happened mostly offline.
Q10: Most of my exam prep wouldn't show up at all in the LMS. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes, doing practice problems on paper, and even explaining concepts out loud to myself—that's how I learn best as a kinesthetic person. Group study sessions with classmates happened over coffee, not through the platform. The LMS only captured a tiny fraction of the work I actually put in.
Q11: That was basically my standard approach. I'd download everything in one session—maybe spend 20 minutes grabbing all the week's materials—then study from those files for days without logging back in. It was more efficient for me and didn't require constant internet access, which mattered when I was studying at places without reliable wifi. The system would show one brief login, but that represented hours of subsequent offline work.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough when everything piled up across all four courses simultaneously. I felt completely bored and detached at times, just going through the motions without really caring about the content. Financial stress was always in the background, making it hard to focus on academic work when real-world problems felt more pressing. The mindfulness exercises actually helped me push through those periods without completely checking out.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. I'd do the bare minimum to keep up with assignments but wouldn't explore anything extra. Competing responsibilities meant I had to be ruthlessly efficient—logging in only when absolutely necessary and spending as little time on the platform as possible while still getting things done.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing while cooking dinner or doing chores, technically watching but not absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through readings without really processing the information because my mind was elsewhere. Those sessions showed up as engagement in the system, but mentally I was checked out. It was especially bad when I was bored with the material or stressed about other things.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts and peer interactions because they felt less urgent than assignments. Even when I knew participating would help my understanding, I'd push it off in favor of things with hard deadlines. Sometimes I avoided starting big assignments because the thought of them felt overwhelming when I was already tired. The mindfulness practice helped me recognize when I was avoiding things and gently redirect myself.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be relatively quiet on the platform until about a week before something was due, then my activity would spike dramatically. I'd download materials, check requirements multiple times, and submit right before the deadline. Without that external pressure, I struggled to motivate myself to engage with the course content proactively.
Q17: Getting good feedback was validating and made me feel like my approach was working, even if it wasn't conventional. When I got my assignment scores back and they were strong, it reinforced that my offline study methods were effective despite low platform engagement. Constructive criticism would make me spend a bit more time on similar assignments in the future. But honestly, feedback came too late to change my habits much within the same course.
Q18: The structure gave me clear milestones to work toward, which helped with my deadline-driven approach. Having assignments spaced throughout the semester meant I couldn't completely ignore the course for weeks at a time. However, the structure also assumed consistent weekly engagement that didn't match my actual life circumstances. I appreciated flexibility in when I could access materials, even if I didn't use the platform as intended.
Q19: Downloadable materials made everything easier—I could work on my own terms without being tethered to the platform. The assignment submission process was straightforward, which reduced friction when I was rushing. What made it harder was when materials required constant online access or when discussion participation was mandatory on specific timelines that didn't align with my schedule. Synchronous elements would have been nearly impossible for me.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious about the course and keeps checking everything obsessively without actually producing work. Or maybe they're struggling to understand the material, so they're watching videos repeatedly but can't translate that into completed assignments. It could also be someone who's going through a hard time personally—they're showing up to the platform but can't follow through. High activity doesn't necessarily mean productive activity.
Q21: That's essentially my experience. I performed well because I studied effectively offline, using methods that suited my kinesthetic learning style. The LMS was just a delivery mechanism for materials, not where my actual learning happened. Someone could easily look at my login frequency and think I wasn't trying, but my assignment scores and exam results tell a different story. Platform engagement and actual learning engagement aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone submitted work, how often they logged in, and which resources they clicked on. But they completely miss the quality of attention during those sessions, offline studying, peer collaboration outside the platform, and the mental effort someone puts into understanding material. They also can't capture motivation, stress levels, or why someone's patterns look the way they do. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: For me, being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply the material, regardless of where or how that happens. It's about mental investment—actually caring about learning something rather than just checking boxes. I felt engaged when I was working through problems by hand, making connections between concepts, and seeing how things applied to real situations. It's not about time spent on a platform; it's about the quality of your mental presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned or how hard I worked outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my handwritten notes, my offline practice sessions, or the conversations with classmates that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show that despite feeling bored and having low motivation, I pushed through and earned a Merit grade through sheer determination and efficient studying. My stress levels, financial constraints, and the effort it took to balance everything—none of that appears in the data.
